title: 18" Doll Beverage Stand
summary: A miniature lemonade-stand counter sized for American Girl-style 18" dolls — pocket-hole boxes, a jigsaw-cut awning, and a bevel-cut roof pitch.
description:
This is a scaled-down version of the full-size lemonade stand elsewhere in the catalog, built entirely from scrap 1x2, 1x3, and 1x4 — the kind of project that clears out the offcut bin. Two boxy counter units join at an angle, each with a window-sill style top, and a bevel-cut roof cap pulls the whole thing together into a little storefront.

The only technique that isn't pure box-building is the pair of 30° bevels on the roof cap pieces: cut those on the miter saw with the piece held flat, not tipped up, and keep fingers well clear since the parts are small. Everything else is pocket-hole joinery sized down, so the skills transfer directly from full-size furniture — just slower, because the pieces are tiny and easy to mis-clamp.

Signs and an awning cut from thin plywood scrap are what make this read as a "stand" rather than two boxes. Keep them thin (1/4" or less) so they don't look chunky next to everything else.

tools.note: Every joint on this is a 3/4" pocket hole — small stock, so clamp it, don't just hold it.
For the 30° bevel cuts on the roof cap. Cut long, then trim to length — a 4" piece is too short to safely start from.
Softens the counter corners and cuts the awning/sign shapes from plywood scrap.
For the counters, awning, and signs — faster and less splitting risk than nails on stock this thin.

materials.name: Pine, 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Scraps are fine — nothing on this project needs a full clean board.

cutList.note: One end cut at 30°, long-point measurement.
Both ends at 30°, not parallel — long point to long point.

steps.title: Build the two wall units
steps.body:
Drill 3/4" pocket holes in the ends of the 10" and 7-1/2" pieces. Glue and pocket-screw them into two flat wall frames — the counter fronts and the back wall. Keep everything square while the glue sets; a small assembly racks out of square easily if you don't clamp it.

Join the two wall units together with pocket holes and glue once both are square.

steps.title: Soften the counter corners
steps.body:
Trace a gentle curve on the front corners of the window sill and the second counter piece, then cut with the jigsaw. Sharp square corners on a toy at kid height are the first thing that gets bumped into — round them over.

Nail and glue the counters to the top of the wall units. Hot glue also works if you'd rather skip the brad nailer for this step.

steps.title: Add the roof cap
steps.body: Glue and nail the four bevel-cut roof cap pieces on top, mitered corners meeting at 30°. Dry-fit before gluing — these pieces are small enough that a bad angle is easy to miss until you're trying to force a gap closed.

steps.title: Cut and attach the awning and signs
steps.body: Cut an awning shape and a couple of small signs from the 1/4" plywood scrap. Hot glue or brad-nail them to the front — this is the detail that turns two boxes into a recognizable little stand, so take a few extra minutes to get the awning shape looking intentional.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand every edge smooth, especially the jigsaw-cut curves. Paint or leave raw — either way, this is small enough to finish in one sitting.

images.alt: 18-inch doll beverage stand with counter and awning
